file_name: Tan et al. - 2023 - Skin cancer image recognition based on similarity clustering and attention transfer.json

did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper uses deep metric learning for similarity clustering and attention transfer (a knowledge distillation technique) to develop an automatic skin cancer recognition framework. This AI-based method was applied to classify skin lesions (melanoma, BCC, SCC, etc.) using the HAM10000 and BCN2000 datasets from the ISIC-2019 collection.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental work developing a new computer-aided diagnosis scheme using deep metric learning and attention transfer. It describes specific methodologies (similarity clustering, knowledge distillation), datasets used (ISIC-2019, HAM10000, BCN2000), implementation details (Pytorch, ResNet, RTX 3090), and reports quantitative results (accuracy, F1-scores) comparing the proposed method against baselines.

title: Skin cancer image recognition based on similarity clustering and attention transfer
journal: Journal of X-Ray Science and Technology
brief_summary: This study developed a computer-aided diagnosis scheme for skin cancer recognition using deep metric learning for similarity clustering and attention transfer for knowledge distillation. The framework was evaluated on the ISIC-2019 dataset, specifically utilizing the HAM10000 and BCN2000 subsets, to classify eight categories of skin lesions including melanoma, basal cell carcinoma, and squamous cell carcinoma. The proposed method achieved a highest validation accuracy of 0.7253, which was 5 percentage points higher than the baseline of 0.6794. In test sets, the method attained accuracies of 0.68 for HAM and 0.53 for BCN, outperforming the respective baselines. Additionally, the F1-scores for malignant lesions BCC, SCC, and MEL improved significantly in both validation and test phases compared to the baseline model. The results demonstrate that integrating similarity clustering with attention transfer effectively enhances the accuracy and F1-score of skin lesion diagnosis.

lesion_types_studied.details: Malignant class included in the 8-category classification task on HAM10000 and BCN2000 datasets. Reported F1-scores: 0.58 (validation), 0.45 (HAM test), 0.55 (BCN test).

Benign class included in the 8-category classification task on HAM10000 and BCN2000 datasets. Reported F1-scores: 0.44 (validation).
Benign class included in the 8-category classification task on HAM10000 and BCN2000 datasets. Reported F1-scores: 0.46 (validation).
Benign class included in the 8-category classification task on HAM10000 and BCN2000 datasets. Reported F1-scores: 0.42 (validation).

all_task_types.target: 8 categories of skin lesions (Basal cell carcinoma, Melanoma, Squamous cell carcinomas, Actinic keratosis, Benign keratosis, Dermatofibroma, Nevus, Vascular)
all_task_types.details: The paper develops a computer-aided diagnosis scheme for skin cancer recognition. The model predicts discrete diagnostic categories (8 classes) using deep metric learning for similarity clustering and attention transfer for knowledge distillation. The final diagnosis corresponds to the maximum probability value from the probability distribution over clinical classes. Performance is evaluated using accuracy and F1-scores for these discrete classes.

datasets_used.name: ISIC-2019 Dataset
datasets_used.description: A collection of quality controlled dermatoscopic images of skin lesions provided by the International Skin Imaging Collaboration (ISIC). It comprises three main subsets: HAM10000 (HAM), BCN2000 (BCN), and MSK, along with an 'unk' (unknown) category.

use.training: HAM and BCN subsets were merged for training and validation stages. MSK and unk were excluded from the final recognition experiments.
use.validation: HAM and BCN subsets were merged for validation stages to evaluate model selection.
use.testing: HAM and BCN subsets were used separately for testing.
use.other: MSK was excluded from similarity clustering and attention transfer experiments due to small quantity.

datasets_used.name: HAM10000 (HAM)
datasets_used.description: A subset of the ISIC-2019 dataset containing dermatoscopic images of skin lesions.

use.training: Used for training the attention transfer model (merged with BCN).
use.testing: Used for independent testing of the proposed framework.

datasets_used.name: BCN2000 (BCN)
datasets_used.description: A subset of the ISIC-2019 dataset containing dermatoscopic images of skin lesions.

use.training: Used for training the attention transfer model (merged with HAM).
use.testing: Used for independent testing of the proposed framework.

datasets_used.name: MSK
datasets_used.description: A subset of the ISIC-2019 dataset.

use.other: Excluded from similarity clustering and attention transfer experiments due to relatively small quantity.

datasets_used.name: UNK
datasets_used.description: Data in the ISIC-2019 dataset that is not part of the HAM, BCN, or MSK main datasets.

use.other: Excluded from similarity clustering and attention transfer experiments.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report any formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. Performance comparisons between the proposed method and baselines were made using absolute differences in accuracy and F1-scores without statistical significance testing.

Validation Set (HAM + BCN merged).notes: The proposed method improved F1-scores for malignant lesions BCC, SCC, and MEL compared to the baseline.

HAM Test Set.notes: F1 scores for BCC and MEL in the proposed method were higher than the baseline. SCC F1 score was lower than baseline but reported as part of the malignant lesion analysis.

BCN Test Set.notes: F1 scores for BCC, SCC, and MEL in the proposed method were higher than the baseline results.

architecture_used.main_model: ResNet (Deep Residual Learning for Image Recognition)
architecture_used.pretraining: Not explicitly reported; the paper states the common classification method of ResNet was considered as baseline and the teacher model weights were utilized from the similarity clustering stage, but does not specify ImageNet or other standard pretraining.
architecture_used.input_size: 224 x 224 (images were uniformly resized to this dimension)

architecture_details.methodology: The framework integrates deep metric learning for similarity clustering (unsupervised) and attention transfer (knowledge distillation). The teacher model (metric teacher model, MTM) is frozen during the student model (classification student model, CSM) training. The student model mimics the attention maps of the teacher model.
architecture_details.attention_transfer_layers: Experiments tested combinations of layers 1, 2, 3, and 4 for attention transfer. The best validation accuracy was achieved using layers 1, 2, and 3.

hyperparameters.epochs: 100 epochs for both stages (similarity clustering and attention transfer)
hyperparameters.attention_transfer_loss: LAT = L(Ws, x) + ||Qs_j - QT_j||^2, where L is cross-entropy loss, Qs and QT are normalized attentional feature maps of student and teacher models, and beta is a hyperparameter (value not specified).

training_details.loss_function: Cross-entropy loss for classification; Deep metric learning loss for clustering; Attention Transfer Loss (LAT) combining cross-entropy and feature map difference for distillation.
training_details.dataset_split: HAM and BCN subsets merged for training and validation; tested separately on HAM and BCN test sets.

classification_or_output_head.type: Softmax probability distribution over 8 clinical classes
classification_or_output_head.decision_rule: Final diagnosis corresponds to the maximum probability value from the probability distribution.

architecture_used.approximate_parameters: Not explicitly reported; the paper notes that 'the number of parameters of both models is kept consistent' between teacher and student models.

machine_learning_or_deep_learning_models: ResNet (Deep Residual Learning for Image Recognition)
preprocessing_techniques: Resize: Uniformly resized images to 224 x 224 pixels for efficient numerical computing and to avoid information redundancy.

Similarity Clustering: Unsupervised learning process using deep metric learning and K-means to extract features with high degree of similarity and gather them into specific clusters (16 clusters used).

Black Edge Removal: Traditional image processing method involving Otsu threshold segmentation, morphological binary operations, and contour extraction to remove black edges from images, specifically applied to 3506 images in clusters containing such artifacts.
Data Partitioning: Conducted based on clustering results to ensure all 8 categories of skin lesion images were present in both training and validation sets.
conclusions: The similarity clustering method based on deep metric learning effectively extracts related feature information to gather similar skin lesion images together.

The proposed classification framework, which integrates similarity clustering with attention transfer (knowledge distillation), improves the total accuracy and F1-score of skin lesion diagnosis compared to baseline methods.
In validation sets, the proposed method achieved a highest accuracy of 0.7253, which is 5 percentage points higher than the baseline accuracy of 0.6794.

The F1-scores for the three malignant lesions (BCC, SCC, and MEL) increased in the validation set (from 0.65 to 0.73, 0.28 to 0.37, and 0.54 to 0.58, respectively) and remained higher than baseline results in both HAM and BCN test sets.
In the HAM test set, the proposed method achieved an accuracy of 0.68, outperforming the baseline of 0.649.
In the BCN test set, the proposed method achieved an accuracy of 0.53, outperforming the baseline of 0.516.

The study demonstrates that combining unsupervised similarity clustering with attention transfer is an effective strategy for enhancing the recognition of skin cancers, particularly for distinguishing malignant lesions with high visual similarity.
important_remarks: The study lacks external validation beyond the two subsets (HAM and BCN) of the ISIC-2019 dataset; the MSK subset was excluded due to small sample size, limiting generalizability assessment.
No statistical significance testing (e.g., p-values) was performed to validate the reported performance improvements over the baseline.

The model achieved low F1-scores for rare malignant classes, specifically Squamous Cell Carcinoma (SCC) with F1-scores of 0.20 (HAM test) and 0.14 (BCN test), indicating poor performance on this specific cancer type.
The dataset exhibits significant class imbalance, with Nevus (NV) comprising 12,875 samples compared to only 628 for Squamous Cell Carcinoma (SCC) in the total ISIC-2019 collection.
The paper does not report the number of model parameters, only stating that teacher and student models were kept consistent in size.
Hyperparameter beta for the attention transfer loss function was not specified in the text.
The baseline accuracy (0.6794) is notably lower than typical state-of-the-art results for this dataset, raising questions about the comparability of the proposed method's absolute performance.
Data leakage risk exists in the preprocessing step where black edge removal was applied based on clustering results, potentially influencing the subsequent data partitioning and training process.
